config.heroHeadline: I want calmer anxiety support. What does the evidence actually support?
config.orientationHeading: Start by naming the anxiety pattern
config.orientationSubtext: The same ingredient can look useful or weak depending on generalized tension, situational triggers, social pressure, physical symptoms, and sleep interference.
config.safetyHeading: Anxiety-support decisions change when risk context changes
config.safetyBody: Do not use supplements to mask persistent panic disorders, severe anxiety, medication interactions, or other complex clinical situations.

anticipatory_worry.title: Anticipatory worry
anticipatory_worry.description: Future-focused worry patterns that escalate before anticipated events or decisions.

physical_tension.title: Physical tension
physical_tension.description: Somatic anxiety patterns including muscle tightness, shallow breathing, or restlessness.

racing_thoughts.title: Racing thoughts
racing_thoughts.description: Mental loops, intrusive thoughts, or cognitive over-activity that worsen anxiety.

situational_nervousness.title: Situational nervousness
situational_nervousness.description: Nervousness or anxiety tied to specific events, triggers, or performance situations.

claims.claim_statement: L-theanine is a plausible first-pass option when anxiety is situational, acute, or tied to performance pressure.
claims.evidence_summary: Small human studies report reductions in physiological and psychological stress markers after L-theanine exposure, with an acute onset profile.
claims.limitations: The evidence base is small, tasks are artificial, and effects may be subtle in real-world high-pressure settings.
claims.best_fit: Presentation nerves, acute performance anxiety, or stimulation smoothing where sedation is not desired.
claims.not_best_fit: Panic-level symptoms, medication complexity, or situations needing clinical support.

claims.claim_statement: L-theanine may reduce cognitive over-activity or worry-loop patterns associated with racing thoughts.
claims.evidence_summary: L-theanine is associated with increased alpha-wave activity in human EEG studies, correlating with a relaxed but alert mental state.
claims.limitations: Effects on cognitive rumination are indirect; most evidence focuses on relaxation or stress markers rather than thought patterns directly.
claims.best_fit: Evening rumination or mild cognitive tension where sedation is not desired.
claims.not_best_fit: Diagnosed anxiety disorders, medication review contexts, or severe panic presentations.

claims.claim_statement: Ashwagandha has the clearest anxiety fit when the pattern is anticipatory or ongoing worry rather than acute event-linked nervousness.
claims.evidence_summary: Randomized trials in adults with generalized anxiety or perceived stress report improvements on anxiety scale scores, with some cortisol-related signals.
claims.limitations: Trials vary by extract, dose, population, and sponsorship; safety context matters more for anxiety than broad adaptogen framing suggests.
claims.best_fit: Persistent anticipatory or trait-level worry where a delayed-onset adaptogen experiment is acceptable.
claims.not_best_fit: Pregnancy, breastfeeding, liver concerns, autoimmune or thyroid complexity, or bipolar-spectrum risk without clinician guidance.

claims.claim_statement: Magnesium is most decision-useful for anxiety when somatic tension, muscle tightness, or low-intake signals are part of the presentation.
claims.evidence_summary: Reviews suggest possible anxiolytic and somatic tension benefits in low-magnesium or combined-nutrient contexts; physical anxiety symptoms may respond to magnesium status correction.
claims.limitations: Benefits are not uniform, many studies use combinations, and form, dose, kidney status, and gastrointestinal tolerance matter.
claims.best_fit: A conservative first-step when physical tension or low dietary magnesium is plausible.
claims.not_best_fit: Kidney disease, severe or clinical anxiety, or expectations of direct sedation.

claims.claim_statement: Passionflower is a plausible option when racing thoughts or cognitive restlessness dominate the anxiety pattern.
claims.evidence_summary: Small human RCTs in generalized anxiety and pre-procedure contexts report comparable improvement to low-dose benzodiazepines, with sedation as a noted effect.
claims.limitations: Evidence base is small and studies are dated; sedation risk and surgical or drug interaction contexts require careful review.
claims.best_fit: Cognitive or mental arousal-dominant anxiety where a sedative effect is acceptable.
claims.not_best_fit: Pregnancy, breastfeeding, pre-surgical settings, CNS medication complexity, or known sedative sensitivity.

claims.claim_statement: Lemon balm is a mild first-step option for situational nervousness where a strong sedating effect is not desired.
claims.evidence_summary: Human studies report modest reductions in self-reported anxiety and mood-related stress at typical extract doses, with a tolerability profile supportive of low-stakes use.
claims.limitations: Evidence is limited and studies are small; effects may be too subtle for moderate-to-severe presentations.
claims.best_fit: Low-stakes situational anxiety where tolerability and mild calming are priorities.
claims.not_best_fit: Clinical-level anxiety, medication complexity, or situations where sedation risk is a concern.

claims.claim_statement: Kava has the strongest acute anxiolytic evidence among the botanical options but carries a liver safety signal that changes the risk calculus.
claims.evidence_summary: Meta-analyses and systematic reviews report significant anxiolytic effects in generalized anxiety, with effect sizes comparable to pharmaceutical comparators in some studies.
claims.limitations: Liver toxicity reports -- though debated -- require clinical safety screening before use; long-term safety is not established for regular use.
claims.best_fit: Short-term situational anxiety in adults who have screened liver status and reviewed medication interactions.
claims.not_best_fit: Liver disease, alcohol use, CNS depressant combinations, pregnancy, or chronic use without medical guidance.

safetyNotes.warning: Rare liver-injury reports and immunomodulatory activity make liver disease, autoimmune conditions, and thyroid medication contexts relevant safety checks.
safetyNotes.decision_effect: Pause self-directed use when autoimmune disease, thyroid complexity, or liver risk context is present.

safetyNotes.warning: Pregnancy and breastfeeding are poor contexts for self-directed ashwagandha use.
safetyNotes.decision_effect: Do not use ashwagandha as a self-directed anxiety experiment in pregnancy or breastfeeding.

safetyNotes.warning: Rare but serious liver injury reports have been documented with kava use, including cases of liver failure.
safetyNotes.decision_effect: Avoid self-directed kava use when liver disease, abnormal liver labs, or history of liver injury is present; screen liver status before use.

safetyNotes.warning: Kava potentiates CNS depressants, alcohol, and sedating medications in an additive or synergistic way.
safetyNotes.decision_effect: Avoid combining kava with alcohol, benzodiazepines, opioids, or other sedating agents.

safetyNotes.warning: Stacking L-theanine with sedatives, alcohol, or multiple calming agents can make effects less predictable.
safetyNotes.decision_effect: Avoid stacking until the single-ingredient response and medication context are clear.

safetyNotes.warning: Lemon balm has mild sedative properties and may interact additively with other calming or sedating agents.
safetyNotes.decision_effect: Avoid stacking lemon balm with alcohol, benzodiazepines, or other sedating supplements until single-ingredient response is established.

safetyNotes.warning: Kidney disease changes magnesium handling and can make supplementation unsafe without clinician guidance.
safetyNotes.decision_effect: Do not use magnesium as a self-directed anxiety experiment with kidney disease.

safetyNotes.warning: Passionflower is contraindicated in pregnancy; sedative properties also require review before combining with CNS medications.
safetyNotes.decision_effect: Do not use passionflower during pregnancy; review CNS medication combinations before use.

l-theanine-situational-nervousness.title: L-Theanine reduces psychological and physiological stress responses
l-theanine-situational-nervousness.source_note: Small human acute stress task study; supporting evidence for acute situational anxiety framing.

l-theanine-racing-thoughts.title: Effects of L-theanine administration on stress-related symptoms and cognitive functions in healthy adults
l-theanine-racing-thoughts.source_note: Human trial with mental health and sleep-quality measures; supports cognitive tension and worry-loop framing.

ashwagandha-anticipatory-worry.title: A prospective, randomized double-blind, placebo-controlled study of safety and efficacy of a high-concentration full-spectrum extract of ashwagandha root in reducing stress and anxiety in adults
ashwagandha-anticipatory-worry.source_note: Randomized trial in stressed adults using validated anxiety and stress scales; strong methodological basis for anticipatory worry framing.

magnesium-physical-tension.title: The effects of magnesium supplementation on subjective anxiety and stress
magnesium-physical-tension.source_note: Systematic review of subjective anxiety and stress evidence; highlights physical tension and low-intake subgroup signals.

passionflower-racing-thoughts.title: Passionflower in the treatment of generalized anxiety: a pilot double-blind randomized controlled trial with oxazepam
passionflower-racing-thoughts.source_note: Small RCT comparing passionflower to oxazepam in generalized anxiety; supports racing-thoughts and cognitive-restlessness framing.

lemon-balm-situational-nervousness.title: Pilot trial of Melissa officinalis L. leaf extract in the treatment of volunteers suffering from mild-to-moderate anxiety disorders and sleep disturbances
lemon-balm-situational-nervousness.source_note: Pilot human trial of lemon balm for mild-to-moderate anxiety; modest effect size supports low-stakes situational use.

kava-situational-nervousness.title: Kava extract for treating anxiety
kava-situational-nervousness.source_note: Cochrane-style systematic review of kava for anxiety; provides the clearest aggregate evidence for anxiolytic effect.
